questions.question: Which of the following reactions is an example of a redox reaction?
questions.explanation:
The reaction in which oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously are termed as a redox reaction.
{tex}\mathop {{\text{Xe}}}\limits^{ + 4} {{\text{F}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + }}\mathop {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits^{ + 1} {\text{(}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{)}} \to \mathop {{\text{Xe}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{6}}}}\limits^{ + 6\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits^{0\,\,\,\,} {/tex}
Since Xe undergoes oxidation while O undergoes reduction. So, it is an example of a redox reaction.

questions.question: In which of the following compounds, nitrogen exhibits highest oxidation state?
questions.explanation:
N2H4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 4(+1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2
NH3 = x + 3(+1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -3
N3H = 3x + 1(+1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1/3

questions.question: The maximum oxidation state shown by any p-block element is:

options.option: Half of the sum of the s and p-electrons
The sum of the s and p-electrons
The sum of the s electrons

questions.explanation: The maximum oxidation state of any p block element is the number of ionizable electrons which is equal to the sum of s and p electrons present in the valence shell. For example, the valance shell electronic configuration of group 15 element is ns2np3. So the maximum oxidation state is +5.

questions.question: The sum of the s and p-electrons is called:

options.option: The most unstable valence number of p-block elements
The group oxidation state of p-block elements
The most stable valence number of p-block elements

The group oxidation state is the highest oxidation state exhibited by the elements present in any particular group.
For p block elements it is the sum of the s and p-electrons.

questions.question: The inert pair effect of p-block elements is due to the:

options.option: the group oxidation state of p-block elements.
the inertness of the inner ns electrons because of ineffective shielding.
the most unstable valence number of p-block elements.
the most stable valence number of p-block elements.

questions.explanation:
The inert pair effect is nothing but the extra stability concept. Generally, P-block elements show variable valiancy which is equal to the maximum oxidation (Group oxidation state) state of that group of p-block elements, p block elements may show another oxidation state i.e.+2.
Group oxidation state +4 is obtained when electrons are lost from both ns and np levels, +2 oxidation state is obtained while only np level electrons are lost due to extra stability and poor shielding of ns electron which are called inert pair. The effect is the inert effect.

questions.question:
The correct statements among I to III regarding group 13 element oxides are:
I. Boron trioxide is acidic.
II. Oxides of aluminium and gallium are amphoteric.
III. Oxides of indium and thallium are basic.
questions.explanation: All the given statements are correct. For group 13 elements, the acidic nature of oxides decreases and the basic nature of oxides increases on moving from B to Tl. This is because as we move down the group, the atomic size of elements goes on increasing, whereas the ionization energy decreases, due to which the strength of the metal oxide (MO) bond goes on decreasing. Thus, boron trioxide or boron oxide is acidic and reacts with basic oxides to give metal borates. Aluminum and gallium oxides are amphoteric while oxides of indium and thallium are basic in nature.

questions.question: The relative stability of +1 oxidation state of group 13 elements follows the order
questions.explanation:
The stability order of + 3 and + 1 oxidation states of group 13 elements will be:
B3+ > Al3+ > Ga3+ > In3+ >> Tl3+ (order of + 3 oxidation state)
B+ << Al+ < Ga+ < In+ < Tl+ (order of + 1 oxidation state)
The presence of two oxidation states in p-block elements is due to the inert pair effect.
Because of the presence of poor shielding d and f -orbitals, as we move from Ga to Tl, an effective nuclear charge of these elements increases so as to hold the valence ns2 electrons tightly. It causes difficult to the ionization of ns2-electrons and it remains inert, only np1-electron ionizes to give + 1 oxidation state.

questions.question: The electronegativity of aluminium is similar to

Be and Al show diagonal relationship which is based on their same {tex}\frac{Z^{*}}{r}{/tex} value (Z* is an effective nuclear charge, r = atomic radius). So, they have similar electronegativity.

questions.question: The increasing order of atomic radii of the following Group 13 elements is

questions.question: Ionisation enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}iH1 kJ mol-1) for the elements of Group 13 follows the order.
questions.explanation:
On moving down the group from B to TI, a regular decreasing trend in the ionisation energy values is not observed.

B Al Ga In Tl
801 577 579 558 589 KJ mol-1

In Ga, there are ten d-electrons in the penultimate shell that screen the nuclear charge less effectively and thus, outer electron is held firmly. As a result, the ionisation energy of both Al and Ga is nearly the same i.e 577 and 579. The increase in ionisation energy from In to Tl is due to poor screening effect of 14f electrons present in the inner shell in Tl.

questions.question: The tendency of BF3, BCl3 and BBr3 to behave as Lewis acid decreases in the sequence:
questions.explanation:
The relative Lewis acid character of boron trihalides is found to follow the following order,
BBr3 > BCl3 > BF3
but the expected order on the basis of electronegativity of the halogens (electronegativity of halogens decreases from F to I) should be,
BF3 > BCl3 > BBr3
This anomaly is explained on the basis of the relative tendency of the halogen atom to back donate its unutilized electrons to the vacant p-orbital of the boron atom. In BF3, boron has a vacant 2p-orbital and each fluorine has fully filled unutilized 2p-orbitals. Fluorine transfers two electrons to vacant 2 p-orbital of boron, thus forming p{tex}\pi{/tex}-p{tex}\pi{/tex} bond.

This type of bond has some double-bond character and is known as dative or back bonding. All three bond lengths are the same. It is possible when a double bond is delocalized. The delocalization may be represented as:

The tendency to back donate decreases from F to I as the energy level difference between B and halogen atom increases from F to I. So, the order is
BF3 < BCl3 < BBr3

questions.question: Ionisation enthalpy ({tex}\Delta_1{/tex}H1 kJ mol-1) for the elements of Group 13 follows the order:
questions.explanation:
On moving down the group from B to Tl, a regular decreasing trend in the I.E. is not observed.

Element B Al Ga In Tl
I.E. (kJ/mol)  801 577 579 558 589

In Ga, there are ten d-electrons in the penultimate shell which screen the nuclear charge less effectively and thus, outer electron is held firmly. As a result, the ionisation energy of both Al and Ga is nearly the same. The increase in ionisation energy from In to Tl is due to poor screening effect of 14f electrons present in the inner shell.
Thus, the correct order is, B > Al < Ga > In < Tl

questions.question: The increasing order of atomic radii of the following group 13 elements is:
questions.explanation:
The increasing order of atomic radii of group 13 elements is:
Ga (135 pm) < Al (143 pm) < In (167 pm) < Tl(170pm)
On moving down the group, the atomic radii increases. However, due to poor shielding of the nuclear charge by 3d electrons, {tex}\underset {(135pm)}{r_{Ga}}​​ < \underset{(143pm)}{r_{Al}​ } {/tex}Ga has 10d electrons which do not screen the nuclear charge in an effective manner.
The electrons in the outermost shell of Ga experience more force of attraction towards the nucleus than Al which decreases atomic radius.

questions.question:
Identify the element that has higher first ionization energy, from each of the following pairs,

  1. Be and B
  2. B and Al
  3. Al and Si
  4. In and TI
questions.explanation:
In case of (i), Be (s-block) has higher first ionization energy than B (p-block) because of the smaller size and higher effective nuclear charge in the former. Be has s-orbital [He]2s2 which is completely filled and there are no unpaired electrons in its p-orbitals.
In case of B, there is a high tendency to lose one unpaired electron which is present in its 2p-orbital because p-electrons are held less strongly than s-electrons.
In case of (ii), the decrease in ionization enthalpy from B to AI is associated with an increase in size.
In case of (iii), there is an increase in ionization enthalpy across the period i.e. AI to Si as there is an increase in effective nuclear charge.
In case of (iv), TI has higher ionization enthalpy than In due to the inert pair effect.

questions.question: Which one of the following compounds is not a protonic acid?

B(OH)3 in the aqueous medium coordinates a molecule of water to form the hydrated species. In this species, B3+ ion, because of its small size, exercises a high polarizing power thereby pulling the sigma electron charge of the coordinated O atom towards itself. The coordinated oxygen, in turn, pulls the sigma electron charge of the OH bond of the attached water molecule towards itself. This facilitates the removal of H+ ion from the O—H bond.

Thus, the solution of B(OH)3 in water acts as a weak acid, and it is not a protonic acid.

questions.question: In the following reaction sequences, X, Y, Z are ________ respectively.

In the structure of diborane, H-B-H and B-H-B bonds are respectively known as ________.

  1. 2c and 3c covalent bonds.
  2. 2c-2e and 3c covalent bonds.
  3. 2c-2e covalent and 3c-2e banana bonds.
  4. 3c-2e banana and 2c-2e covalent bonds.
questions.explanation:
1. Four terminal B-H bonds are normal covalent bonds and are quite strong, they are also called “two centre electron-pair bonds” or “two centre two-electron bonds” (2c-2e).
  2. The two bridge bonds i.e. B-H-B are quite different from normal covalent bonds. Each bridged hydrogen is bonded to two boron atoms by a pair of electrons. These are “three centre electron-pair bonds” or “three centre two-electron bonds” (3c-2e) and are also known as “banana bonds”.

In case of heavier elements because of poor shielding of d and f orbitals, effective nuclear charge increases. Thus valence electrons present in an s orbital re more tightly bound to the nucleus, and more energy is needed to ionize those electron. So s-orbital electrons are less likely to be involved in bonding.

questions.question: The correct order of catenation is
questions.explanation:
Catenation property is a unique property of group 14 elements. Down the group 14, catenation power decreases as:
C > Si > Ge {tex}\approx{/tex} Sn
Pb does not show catenation.

questions.question: The element that does not show catenation is
questions.explanation:
The property of self-linking of atoms of an element through covalent bonds to form straight or branched chains and rings of different sizes is called catenation. Down the group, catenation tendency decreases due to a decrease in element bond strength. Carbon (C), silicon (Si), germanium (Ge), tin (Sn), lead (Pb) are group-14 elements.
Catenation tendency is highest in carbon while silicon has the second-highest tendency of catenation among all elements of a family due to higher bond energy. The decreasing tendency of catenation among group 14 elements is as follows:
C >> Si > Ge {tex} \approx {/tex} Sn
However, Pb does not show catenation.

questions.question: The first ionizations enthalpies of Na, Mg, Al, and Si are in the order:
questions.explanation:
As we move across the period, nuclear charge increases, atomic size decreases hence, ionization enthalpy increases. For Al (3s23p1), the electron has to be removed from partially filled 3p orbital whereas, in Mg(3s2), the electron has to be removed from stable fully filled 3s orbital.
Removal of an electron from a stable, fully filled orbital requires more energy than removal of an electron from a partially filled orbital. Thus, ionization enthalpy for Mg is greater than ionization enthalpy for Al. So, the correct order of first ionization enthalpies is: Na < Mg > Al < Si.

questions.question: The stability of dihalides of {tex}{Si}, {Ge}, {Sn}{/tex} and Pb increases steadily in the sequence :
questions.explanation: The stability of dihalides of Si, Ge, Sn, and Pb increases in the order SiX2 < GeX2 < SnX2 < PbX2 due to the increasing metallic character and decreasing electronegativity of the elements as you move down the group in the periodic table. Silicon, being the lightest, forms less stable dihalides because it has a stronger tendency to form covalent bonds. As we move to germanium, tin, and lead, the stability of their dihalides increases due to their larger atomic size and lower electronegativity, which allows for more effective bonding with halogens. Lead, being the heaviest, has the most stable dihalides due to its ability to accommodate larger halide ions and its lower tendency to form covalent bonds compared to the lighter elements. Thus, the order reflects the increasing stability of the dihalides as the atomic number increases.

  • These delocalized electrons are free to move over the layers, which is why graphite conducts electricity and has metallic luster.

  4. Usually, they are resistant to oxidation and used as greases.
questions.topicName: Some Important Compounds of Carbon and Silicon
questions.explanation:
Silicones are polysiloxanes with general chemical formula,
[R2SiO]n, where R is an organic group such as:
— CH3, — C2H5, — C6H5, etc.
Silicones have many useful properties:

  1. They repel water and form watertight seals.
  2. They are heat resistant because of the constancy of properties over a wide range of temperature (- 100° to 250° C).
  3. Silicones are non-toxic.
  4. Silicones are biocompatible because these do not support microbiological growth and these have high gas permeability at room temperature.
  5. They are resistant to O2, O3 and UV-radiation.
  6. Silicones are formulated to be electrically insulative.
  7. Silicone grease is typically used as a lubricant for brake components in automobiles, since it is stable at high temperature, is not water-soluble and is an odourless viscous liquid.

questions.question: The mineral Na2{tex}\mathrm{Fe}_{3}^{\mathrm{II}} \mathrm{Fe}_{2}^{\mathrm{III}}{/tex}[Si8O22](OH)2 (chrocidolite) is a:
questions.topicName: Some Important Compounds of Carbon and Silicon
questions.explanation:
The given mineral  Na2{tex}\mathrm{Fe}_{3}^{\mathrm{II}} \mathrm{Fe}_{2}^{\mathrm{III}}{/tex}[Si8O22](OH)2 is Chrysotile (also known as blue asbestos, a type of amphibole).

  • Amphiboles are a group of chain silicates, which form long chains of silica tetrahedra connected by metal ions, often including water in their structure.
  • The formula structure here indicates a chain silicate because of the specific arrangement of silicon-oxygen tetrahedra, characteristic of the amphibole group.

Thus, it belongs to the amphibole chain silicates category.

questions.question: The method used for obtaining highly pure silicon, used as a semiconductor material, is:
questions.topicName: Some Important Compounds of Carbon and Silicon
questions.explanation:
Pure silicon is made by the reduction of highly purified silicon tetrachloride (SiCl4) with dihydrogen.
SiCl4 + 2H2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Si + 4HCl.
The silicon obtained is purified further by the zone refining process. A method of refining metals some inorganic and organic compounds depending on the difference in solubility of impurities in the liquid and solid states is called the zone-refining process.

questions.question:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

  1. CO2 (g) is used as a refrigerant for ice-cream and frozen food.
  2. The structure of C60 contains twelve six-carbon rings and twenty-five carbon rings.
  3. ZSM-5, a type of zeolite, is used to convert alcohol into gasoline.
  4. CO is a colorless and odorless gas.
questions.topicName: Some Important Compounds of Carbon and Silicon
questions.explanation:
1. CO2​ in solid-state used as a refrigerant for ice-cream and frozen food because of its sublimation properties. Sublimation is a process in which a solid directly goes into the gaseous state without going into the liquid state.
  2. The structure of C60 contains twelve five-carbon rings and twenty-six carbon rings. Hence the given statement was wrong.
  3. ZSM-5, a type of zeolite, is used to convert alcohol into gasoline. The statement is correct.
  4. CO is a colorless and odorless gas. True

One sigma ($\sigma$) and half pi ($\pi$) bond
One sigma ($\sigma$) and two pi ($\pi$) bond

questions.topicName: Some Important Compounds of Carbon and Silicon

A triple bond between two atoms is always considered as a one sigma bond and two pi bonds. So according to the given structure CaC2​ (Calcium carbide) has 1 sigma and 2 pi bonds.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements about the zeolites is false?

  1. They are used as cation exchangers
  2. They have open structure which enables them to take up small molecules
  3. Zeolites are aluminosilicates having three dimensional network
  4. Some of the {tex}\mathrm{SiO}_4^{4-}{/tex}units are replaced by {tex}\mathrm{AIO}_4^{5-}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{AIO}_6^{9-}{/tex} ions in zeolites.
questions.topicName: Some Important Compounds of Carbon and Silicon
questions.explanation: Zeolites are alumino silicates having three dimensional open structure in which four or six membered ring predominates. Thus, due to open chain structure, they have cavities and can take up water and other small molecules.

questions.question:
{tex}{Me}_{2} {SiCl}_{2} \xrightarrow{{H}_{2} {O}}(A) \xrightarrow{\text { condensation }}{/tex} (B).
The nature of product (B) may be
questions.topicName: Some Important Compounds of Carbon and Silicon
questions.explanation: The reaction involves the hydrolysis of dimethylsilicon dichloride, which typically leads to the formation of silanol groups. Upon condensation, these silanol groups can link together to form polymers. In this case, the structure of the starting material suggests that the resulting polymer will primarily be linear due to the nature of the bonding and the sterics involved. Cyclic products are less favored in this scenario because the formation of linear chains is more thermodynamically stable and common for such siloxane reactions. Therefore, the correct answer focuses on the formation of only linear polymers.

questions.question: Producer gas is the mixture of :
questions.topicName: Some Important Compounds of Carbon and Silicon
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because producer gas primarily consists of carbon monoxide (CO) and nitrogen (N2). It is generated by the partial combustion of carbonaceous materials, such as coal, in a limited supply of air. While hydrogen (H2) can also be present in small amounts, the main components are CO and N2.
